title: Web Scalability for Startup Engineers
subtitle: Tips & Techniques for Scaling Your Web Application
author: Artur Ejsmont
description:
A pragmatic handbook for engineers building web systems
that must grow from a few hundred users to many millions without
rewriting from scratch. Ejsmont distills a decade of scaling experience
at Yahoo!, Amazon, and elsewhere into a single coherent playbook:
start with a sensible, layered architecture; keep every service
stateless; cache aggressively at every layer; push work off the
request path with asynchronous queues; design data partitioning
before you need it; measure everything. The book is unusually
respectful of startup constraints — small teams, tight budgets,
incomplete information — and refuses to prescribe enterprise patterns
for problems startups do not yet have.
keyIdeas:
Scalability is the ability to absorb increased load without
proportional cost or rewrite
Horizontal scaling beats vertical scaling at any meaningful size

Statelessness is the single most important property of a scalable
service

Cache at every layer: browser, CDN, reverse proxy, app, object
cache, database
Asynchronous processing removes work from the critical request path

Front-end, web services, and data layers should be scaled
independently

Data partitioning (sharding) must be designed before it becomes
urgent

Eventual consistency is acceptable for far more use cases than
engineers assume

Idempotency is a precondition for safe retries and queue-based
processing

Lean startup scalability: build for the next 10x, not the next
1000x
keyTakeaways:
Scalability, performance, and availability are related but distinct
properties — optimize for the one that actually limits you

A simple three-layer architecture (front-end, services, data)
handles 95% of startup workloads when each layer is stateless and
cacheable

Cache hits at outer layers (browser, CDN) are orders of magnitude
cheaper than cache hits at inner layers (database) — push caching
outward

Functional partitioning (separating services by feature) buys time;
data partitioning (sharding by key) buys scale

Asynchronous processing is the simplest way to convert a slow
synchronous request into a fast one — move work to a queue and a
worker pool

Message queues require idempotent consumers, dead-letter handling,
and monitoring; treat them as infrastructure, not glue code

Premature microservices are more dangerous than a well-factored
monolith — split a service only when its scaling profile diverges

Capacity planning starts with an RPS budget per endpoint and a
latency budget per request — without numbers you cannot make trade-offs

Observability (metrics, logs, tracing) is not optional; you cannot
scale what you cannot measure
whoShouldRead:
Backend and full-stack engineers at early-stage startups who expect
rapid growth
Tech leads making architecture decisions with limited time and budget

Junior to mid-level engineers who want a single coherent mental
model of web scaling

Engineering managers who need to evaluate scaling proposals from
their teams

Self-taught developers moving from solo projects to production
systems
